titleStmt.title: Despatch from Owen L. W. Smith to David J. Hill, November 6, 1900

distributor.name: Center for Digital Research in the Humanities

availability.p: Licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 3.0 Unported License. Derivatives must be credited to To Enter Africa from America, made available non-commercially, and distributed under the same terms. Requests for permission for commercial publication or other use should be emailed to the project team.

bibl.title: Despatch from Owen L. W. Smith to David J. Hill, November 6, 1900
Despatches from United States Consuls in Monrovia, 1852-1906

correspAction.roleName: U.S. Minister Resident and Consul General

div1.note: Index Bureau Dept of State Rec'd. Dec 8 '00
div1.p:
No. 52. Consular.
Legation of the United States.
Monrovia Liberia,
Nov. 6, 1900.

Hon. David J Hill,
Assistant Secretary of State,
Washington.
Subject.
Correspondence, relative to the estate of the late Auranus M. Miller, late of Harper (Cape Palmas) Liberia

No. 52. "Consular."
Legation of the United States,
Monrovia Liberia,
Nov. 6, 1900.

Hon. David J. Hill,
Assistant Secretary of State,
Washington.
Sir:

Referring to my No. 51, "Consular," Oct. 26, 1900. I have the honor to say that: carefully searching the records and files of this Legation, touching the correspondence between this Legation, the State and other Departments, of the Liberian Government, and other individuals in this country and the United States, relative the estate of the late Auranus M. Miller, deceased, late of Harper, (Cape Palmas) Liberia; I have found the originals, of which copies are
are, herewith, inclosed.

In note No. 1. Oct. 31, 1892, of Wm. D. McKoy Esq. to E. Warneke, giving number of Treasury Orders &c., you will take notice that the apparently erased, No. 75 (No. 74) written in red ink is a faithful copy of the Record, whatever it may represent.

2. I regret to say we find no record of Note of Wm. D. McKoy Esq., remitting the $50 order, acknowledged having been received by Hon. H. A. Williams, Secretary of Treasury, in his note of January 4, 1893, as per inclosed copy.

We, however, are inclined to believe that the $50.– One General Government Order No. 40, undoubtedly, added after the footing up of the several Treasury Orders, amounting to $1001.75; is the $50. order of the
the Hon. H. A. Williams, Secretary of the Treasury Jan. 4, 1893, (as per inclosed copy), which appears to have been added to the original sum. Note No. 1. of Oct. 31, 1892. See Note of Wm. D. McKoy Esq., of March 25, 1893, to Mrs. A. M. Miller, Newcastle, Me., in which he, undoubtedly, refers to the $50. Treasury Order, of the Hon. H. A. Williams, Secretary of the Treasury, Jan. 4, 1893.
We find no record of any correspondence, touching this case, between Wm. H. Heard Esq., and any other party, excepting a receipt from F. O. Thorn, for $456.59, in Treasury Order, Jan. 1898.

3. I inclose, herewith, a correct copy of the Act of the Liberian Legislature, session 1893-4, referred to by Hon. H. A. Williams, Secretary of the Treasury, in his Note
Note, of February 22, 1894 to Vice Consul-General, B. Y. Payne.

Hoping the foregoing may be of some service, as well as information, relative to this case, and thinking it very advisable that F. O. Thorn Esq., who is the senior senator from Maryland county, be required to turn over the Treasury Order for $456.59, (in his possession) to this Legation, for collection, if possible.

I have the honor to be your obedient servant,
Owen L. W. Smith,
M. R. and C. General.
Inclosures.
1. Wm. D. McKoy, No. 1, Oct. 31, 1892, to E. Warneke Esq. Cape Palmas.
2. Wm. D. McKoy, No. 19, Dec. 5, 1892, to Hon. G. W. Gibson, Sec. State.
3. Wm. D. McKoy, Mar. 25, 1893, to Mrs. A. M. Miller, Newcastle, Me.
(see page 7)
4. Hon. H. A. Williams, Jan. 4, 1893, to Wm. D. McKoy.
5. Wm. D. McKoy, No. 23, April 24, 1893, to Hon. G. W. Gibson, Sec. of State.
6. B. Y. Payne, Aug. 19, 1893, to Mrs. E. A. Miller, Newcastle, Me.
7. B. Y. Payne, Feb'y 21, 1894, to Hon. H. A. Williams Sec. Treasury.
8. Hon. H. A. Williams, Sec. Treasury, to B. Y. Payne. Feb'y 22, 1894.
9. F. O. Thorn, Receipt, Jan. 1898, to W. H. Heard.
10. Copy Act of Liberian Legislature, session 1893-4.
Copy

Received from Honorable W. H. Heard Consul General of the United States at Monrovia Liberia, the sum of Four Hundred Fifty Six 59/100 Dollars in Liberia government Bills on a/c Miss Winifred Miller of Newcastle Maine U.S.A.
F. O. Thorne

Monrovia,
January 1898.
Copy

Treasury Department,
Monrovia, Feb'y, 22, 1894.

Hon. B. Y. Payne,
U.S. Vice Consul General,
Monrovia.
Sir:

I have received your note of yesterday, referring to the amt. of $598 15/100 in Maryland County obligations, belonging to the estate of the late A. M. Miller. I beg to say that by a resolution of the last Legislature the Department is authorized to receive in lien of the currency arising from the issue of 3% Bonds &c scrip of all description. If it should suit you, the amt. of obligations in your possession, will be taken up by the Department for currency on account of the next accumulation of this money. Payment otherwise than by this
this means, would have to be by the check of the Superintendent of Maryland county upon the Sub. Treasurer there.

I have the honor
Sir,
to be yours obediently
H. A. Williams.
Copy

Consulate General of the United States,
Monrovia, February 21, 1894.

Hon. H. A. Williams,
Secretary of the Treasury,
Republic of Liberia.
Dear Sir:

Recurring to our interview in reference to the claim of the Estate of Mr. A. M. Miller against your Government, I beg to say, I have in my possession $598.15, in Maryland County obligations, and I will be glad, if you can settle these or advise me what course to pursue to cause an early settlement of same, as his widow is very much in need of the money.

I have the honor to be,
Sir,
Your obedient Servant,
Beverly Y. Payne,
U.S. Vice Consul General.
Copy

Consulate General of the United States,
Monrovia, Liberia, August 19, 1893.
Mrs. E. A. Miller,
Newcastle Maine;
Madame,
I am in receipt of your favor of the 11th ult., inclosing ($15.00) Fifteen dollars in Liberian Currency and soliciting my services in settling your late husband's estate.
I beg to say that your Liberian Government bills are in my possession, and will have my attention as soon as the affairs at Cape Palmas (where they are payable) will warrant.
I shall sell the Liberian Currency ($15.00) for what it will bring in the market, and remit you at an early date.

I have the honor to be,
Madame,
Your ob't Servant,
Beverly Y. Payne,
U.S.V.C.G.
Copy

Legation of the U.S.A.
Monrovia, Liberia, April 24, 1893.

Honorable G. W. Gibson,
Secretary of State,
Monrovia, Liberia.
Sir:

As I shall probably leave this post at no distant day it becomes my duty to make a report upon all unfinished business, and this in time that my Government may further instruct me before I take my departure, in case further instruction be necessary. In accordance herewith I respectfully ask a report upon each of the following subjects numbered 1, 2 and 3:
1. The Treasury claims against Maryland County, belonging to the Estate of Capt. A. M. Miller, deceased.
(See my communication No. 19 bearing date, Dec. 5, 1892.)
(over)

2. The destruction of the Baptist Vic Mission at Bendon. There is hardly a probability of any more goods being recovered and it seems to me all that is necessary now is for your Government to determine the responsibility incumbent upon it, or that it will assume.
3. Whether or not our Government may expect a reduction in Postal Rates between Liberia and the United States, and if so, when? If not, why?
Hoping that these subjects shall receive your earliest attention, I have the honor to be, Sir,–
Your obedient servant, William D. McCoy, U.S. Minister Resident and Consul General.
Copy

Treasury Department,
Monrovia, Jan'y 4, 1893.

Hon'ble W. D. McCoy,
U.S. Minister &c
Sir,
I beg to acknowledge receipt of your note enclosing order for $50. on account of late Capt. Miller.
I retain same and will give same attention by arranging the payment of.

Yours &c.
H. A. Williams,
Sec'y Treas'ry.
Copy

Treasury Department
Secretary of the Treasury.

Republic of Liberia,
Monrovia, Jan'y 4, 1893.

Hon'ble W. D. McCoy,
U.S. Minister &c
Monrovia
Sir,
Agreeably with my note of this morning, I beg to enclose check in payment of the order you presented on account Estate of the late Capt. Miller.

Yours &c
H. A. Williams.
Sec'y Treasury.
Copy

Legation of the U.S.A.
Monrovia, Liberia, March, 25, 1893.

Mrs. A. M. Miller,
Newcastle, Maine.
Madame:–

Affairs at Cape Palmas are in such an unsettled condition, that it is impossible to tell when the Treasurer will be able to cash those orders belonging to the Estate of your deceased husband. I have them on hand and shall do what is possible to get them cashed before I leave this post. In case I am not successful I shall turn the Orders over to my successor, take his receipt for the same and forward a copy of the receipt to you.

I have collected one Government order of $50.00 2/3 being
being paid in current money and 1/3 in Liberian currency. The law authorizes such payment although the currency is almost worthless. I am obliged to send it to you for I cannot dispose of it. Occasionally one can get 50¢ on a dollar for it but it is seldom that there is any demand for it at all.
Please acknowledge the receipt of this directing your letter to me to the Department of State, Washington, D.C.
If I shall have left Monrovia it will be kept there for me.

Very respectfully yours,
William D. McCoy
U.S. Minister Resident and Consul General
(over)
Statement

William D. McCoy,
U.S. Minister Resident and Consul-General.

Legation of the United States,
Monrovia, Liberia, Dec. 5, 1892.

Honorable G. W. Gibson,
Sec. of State of the Republic of Liberia.
Sir:

This will respectfully inform you, that I have Treasury Orders on the Treasurer of Maryland County, Liberia, amounting in value to the sum of Five Hundred Ninety Eight and 15/100 Dollars ($598 15/100) belonging to the Estate of Captain A. M. Miller, late of Cape Palmas but now deceased.

I respectfully request that the proper Officials be instructed to arrange with your department, that you may make a settlement at your earliest convenience, that I may close up the affairs pertaining to the estate.
Inclosed find a list of Registered Numbers together with amounts of credits and dues.
(See pgs. 78 & 79 [see inclosure No. 1, Oct. 31, 1892]).

I am, Sir,
Your obedient servant,
William D. McCoy,
U.S. Minister Resident and Consul General.
Copy

Legation of the United States,
Monrovia, Liberia, Oct. 31, 1892.

E. Warneke Esq.,
Cape Palmas, Liberia,
Sir:
This is to acknowledge the receipt from you, through Mr. Hoenigeberg, of the following Orders belonging to the Estate of the late Captain A. M. Miller:–

Continued
over.
Summary

You will see by the above that the Treasury Orders
= $1001.75 Less Credits
403.60 Balance due
598.15 instead of $599.55 as you made it.
Am very much obliged for your information regarding Mr. James Bowie.

With best wishes for your health and prosperity, I remain
Yours very truly,
William D. McCoy,
U.S. Minister Resident & Consul General.
Transcript
From the Acts of the Legislature of Liberia, Session of 1893-94. Section I, Pgs. 9 & 10.

Sec. 1. That immediately after the passage of this Resolution, the Secretary of the Treasury is directed to exchange the currency on hand in the Treasury Department for redemption of Bonds of 1880, and payment of interest upon the same, or he shall sell the same to the highest bidder, for County audited bills of any county within this Republic, as well as all other currency in any of the Sub-Treasurer's office of this Republic, shall be thus disposed of as above recited; and that the Act passed during the session of 1892-3, authorizing the Secretary of the Treasury to issue three per cent gold bonds in exchange
exchange for deposits of Liberian Currency, be and the same is hereby extended until September 30th, 1895. All currency taken in for exchange of gold bonds within that time shall be included in the provisions of this Joint Resolution.
Any law to the contrary notwithstanding.
Approved January 13th, 1894.
